exemplar.title: An Experiment to Investigate the Correlation Between Articulatory Suppression and the Ability to Recall a Sequence of Letters

detailedExemplarData.title: An Experiment to Investigate the Correlation Between Articulatory Suppression and the Ability to Recall a Sequence of Letters

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Clear statement of aim linked to Landry & Bartling replication
Accurate description of the workingâmemory model and explicit connection to the investigation
Operationalised independent and dependent variables with both null and research hypotheses
weaknesses: Limited realâworld elaboration of the investigationâs relevance
Minor overâsimplification in describing subcomponents of the phonological loop
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strandsâaim and relevance, theory description and connection, and hypothesis/variablesâsecurely meet the 5â6 band descriptors. The aim is stated and its relevance explained, the model is described and linked to the task, and both variables are operationalised. Small lapses in depth and precision justify a 5 rather than 6.

strengths: Independentâmeasures design is identified and its rationale (avoiding order effects) is explained
Opportunity sampling technique is described with basic reasoning about participant availability
Controlled variables and materials are described with some justification for their choice
weaknesses: Sampling rationale is brief and lacks discussion of external validity effects
Limited depth in explaining how controls mitigate specific biases (e.g., fatigue)
Justification of trial reduction and its impact on reliability is not addressed
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Each of the three strands (design, sampling, variables/materials) reaches the 3â4 band by providing explanation rather than mere description. Occasional phrasing issues and surfaceâlevel justifications cap the performance at a midâband score of 3 for Exploration.

strengths: Appropriate use and justification of descriptive (mean, SD) and inferential (MannâWhitney U) statistics
Interpretation of statistical findings linked clearly to the hypothesis and data
Insightful analysis of boxâplot skewness and discussion of distributional shape
weaknesses: Minor conceptual slip in labeling count data as ratio level
Graphical presentation errors: inconsistent decimal notation and mixed use of percentages/raw means
Slight overstatement in pâvalue interpretation (phrasing of probability)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Statistical analysis and interpretation both lie firmly in the 5â6 band: an appropriate nonâparametric test is used, results are reported with justification, and findings are linked to the hypothesis. Graphical presentation contains errors that prevent a perfect 6, yielding a justified score of 5.

strengths: Findings are discussed in relation to the workingâmemory model and original study results
Strengths and limitations are identified and explained in relation to validity and reliability
weaknesses: No concrete modifications are proposed or linked to stated limitations
Evaluation lacks suggested changes (e.g., stratified sampling, trial adjustments)
Additional context (e.g., bilingual instructions) is not elaborated for limitations
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While two strands (findings discussion; strengths and limitations) meet higherâband descriptors, the absence of any proposed modifications prevents reaching the minimum requirements of band 1â2. The overall Evaluation criterion therefore receives a minimal score of 1.

strandSelections.reasoning: A bar chart and separate box plots are provided and clearly relate to the hypothesis.  However, some axes/labels are minimal and percentages vs raw means are mixed, indicating small errors in presentation.  This aligns with the 3-4 descriptor (âaddresses the hypothesis but contains errorsâ); a mid-band 3 is appropriate.

The student explicitly interprets the descriptive pattern and the Mann-Whitney result, links the p-value to rejection of the null hypothesis, and ties the outcome to the predicted effect of articulatory suppression.  Interpretation is accurate and clearly related to both data and hypothesis, fulfilling the 5-6 band; slight overstatement of precision (0.01 % chance) keeps score at 5.

Both a null and a research hypothesis are provided, with the IV (articulatory suppression: repeating â1, 2â twice per second) and the DV (number of letters correctly recalled in order) each operationalised.  The wording makes measurement and manipulation unambiguous, meeting the 5-6 descriptor; minor stylistic issues justify a 5.

Opportunity sampling is identified and the rationale (availability of Year 8 IB students over 16, no parental consent) is given.  Random allocation to conditions is mentioned with a brief caveat on representativeness.  This constitutes an explanation of both technique and choice, matching the 3-4 band; limited depth earns 3.

The working-memory model is described (visuospatial sketchpad, phonological loop, two subcomponents) and linked directly to the current investigation, noting that only the phonological loop is under study.  Articulatory suppression is defined and tied to the dual-task set-up.  The description is largely accurate and the link to the investigation is explicitly drawn, fulfilling the descriptors for the top band; small inaccuracies (e.g., slight over-simplification of component functions) keep the score at 5.

Findings are compared with the original Landry & Bartling results and explicitly linked back to the phonological-loop theory, demonstrating discussion rather than simple description.  Depth is good but not exhaustive, meriting 5 in the top band.

The student clearly states the aim â to investigate whether articulatory suppression affects recall â and explicitly connects it to the Landry & Bartling replication and to Baddeley & Hitchâs working-memory framework.  The paragraph that follows explains why this question matters (it tests the efficiency of the phonological loop and contributes to understanding of dual-task interference).  Because both the aim and its relevance are not only identified but also briefly explained, the work sits best in the 5-6 band; minor brevity and limited real-world elaboration justify a 5 rather than 6.

Multiple strengths (simplicity, monitoring, brief procedure) and limitations (small sample, participant variability) are each explained in relation to potential impact on validity or reliability, satisfying the âexplained and relevantâ criterion of the 5-6 band; breadth is adequate for a score of 5.

Controlled variables (location, time of day, monitoring) and materials (slips, projector, timer) are described and some justification is offered (e.g., quiet room to avoid distraction).  Explanations are present but not consistently detailed, so the strand fits the 3-4 band with a score of 3.

No explicit, justified modifications linked to stated limitations are provided.  As the student merely reports limitations without proposing concrete, justified changes, the work does not reach band 1-2; therefore the score is 0.

The student identifies an independent-measures design and explains why this choice avoided order effects and saved time, showing understanding beyond simple description.  The explanation, while brief, satisfies the âexplainedâ requirement; occasional phrasing issues prevent a full 4.

Descriptive statistics (mean, SD) and an appropriate inferential test (Mann-Whitney U for two independent groups with small, non-parametric data) are applied.  The student justifies the choice and reports p-value, U and Z.  Calculations appear internally consistent; minor conceptual slip in calling the data âratioâ prevents a 6 but still sits securely in the 5-6 band.

annotations.comment: The student effectively integrates a secondary source (Dixon, 2023) for the workingâmemory model, but should cite Baddeley & Hitchâs original paper to strengthen theoretical accuracy.
Aim is stated clearly and linked to the Landry & Bartling replication, demonstrating precise focus on articulatory suppression.
The description of the phonological loop could be more precise: clarify that it stores speechâbased information and distinguish between phonological store and rehearsal more accurately.
The articulation suppression process is defined clearly and linked directly to dualâtask methodology, enhancing conceptual clarity.
The sampling rationale for Year 8 students is only justified by convenience. Expand on how representativeness might affect validity beyond availability.
Consider discussing how reducing trials from ten to three could influence reliability and the comparability of your replication.
Independent and dependent variables are stated clearly, providing unambiguous operational definitions for manipulation and measurement.
Including both null and research hypotheses is commendable; it clarifies the expected direction of articulatory suppression effects.
The independentâmeasures design is well explained with respect to orderâeffect avoidance, demonstrating clear methodological rationale.
Detailed strengths of using independent samples are clearly articulated, enhancing evaluation depth.
Propose concrete modifications to address the small sample size and variability (e.g., stratified sampling, increased trials) linked to limitations.
Mention of language barrier is valuable, but explain how bilingual instructions might influence recall accuracy to contextualize this limitation.
The description of opportunity sampling lacks depth regarding its impact on external validity; consider elaborating on potential sampling bias.
Random assignment by coin flip is noted, but discuss how lack of stratification might introduce group differences in cognitive ability.
Controlling time of day and environment is appropriate, but detail how these controls mitigate specific cognitive fatigue effects.
Materials and timings are described thoroughly, enhancing reproducibility of stimulus presentation and response intervals.
Ethical procedures are noted but could be improved by detailing debrief content and how data confidentiality is preserved.
Classifying count data from 0â7 as ratio is debatedâcounts may be ordinal; justify use of mean/SD versus medians for skewed distributions.
Table 1 uses commas for decimals and lacks units; use consistent decimal notation and label columns with 'Mean (points)' and 'SD (points)'.
The choice of MannâWhitney U is appropriate and justified given small independent samples; this strengthens inferential rigor.
Text mentions 82% and 55% but graph shows raw means; ensure consistency between reported percentages and underlying data metrics.
Interpretation of boxâplot skewness is accurate and insightful, linking distribution shape to participant performance.
The p-value interpretation as 0.01% risk is slightly overstated; clarify that p<.01 indicates strong evidence against Hâ, not exact probability.
Findings are discussed in relation to the workingâmemory model and the original study, demonstrating clear theoretical integration.
When comparing percentage differences with Landry & Bartling, discuss possible causes for the 4% discrepancy (e.g., age, trial number).
